note: Shared picture-book STYLE reference for the on-device style RAG. The parent names books their child loves; EmbeddingGemma retrieves the closest entries and the writer borrows the STYLE (cadence, devices, vocabulary) — never the text. Used by Studio (main bilingual book) and the iPad (Photo Story).

books.title: Brown Bear, Brown Bear, What Do You See?
books.author: Bill Martin Jr. & Eric Carle
books.style: A call-and-response chant: each page asks 'what do you see?' and answers with a brightly colored animal that introduces the next. Predictable, soothing, endlessly repeatable.
books.sample: Brown Bear, Brown Bear, what do you see? I see a red bird looking at me.

books.title: The Very Hungry Caterpillar
books.author: Eric Carle
books.style: A gentle counting tale across the days of the week, naming foods one by one, ending in a hopeful transformation. Simple sentences, sensory food words, a satisfying arc.
books.sample: On Monday he ate through one apple, but he was still hungry.

books.title: Where the Wild Things Are
books.author: Maurice Sendak
books.style: A small child's imagination grows a whole wild world and then sails safely home to love and supper. Lyrical, a little mischievous, big feelings made gentle.
books.sample: And now, cried Max, let the wild rumpus start!

books.title: Goodnight Moon
books.author: Margaret Wise Brown
books.style: A hushed bedtime litany that says goodnight to every little thing in the room. Slow, tender, lulling — perfect for winding down.
books.sample: Goodnight room. Goodnight moon. Goodnight cow jumping over the moon.

books.title: The Gruffalo
books.author: Julia Donaldson & Axel Scheffler
books.style: A bouncy, rhyming romp where a clever little mouse outwits bigger creatures with quick wit. Strong meter, playful rhyme, a brave small hero.
books.sample: A mouse took a stroll through the deep dark wood.

books.title: We're Going on a Bear Hunt
books.author: Michael Rosen & Helen Oxenbury
books.style: A rhythmic outdoor adventure full of sound words as a family wades through grass, river, and mud. Chantable, repetitive, full of motion and onomatopoeia.
books.sample: Swishy swashy! We can't go over it, we can't go under it, we've got to go through it!

books.title: Guess How Much I Love You
books.author: Sam McBratney & Anita Jeram
books.style: A tender bedtime exchange where a little hare and big hare measure their love in playful comparisons. Warm, simple, reassuring.
books.sample: I love you right up to the moon — and back.

books.title: Dear Zoo
books.author: Rod Campbell
books.style: A repeating pattern where the zoo keeps sending the wrong pet until the perfect one arrives. Tiny sentences, animal traits, a satisfying just-right ending.
books.sample: So they sent me a lion. He was too fierce! I sent him back.

books.title: The Snail and the Whale
books.author: Julia Donaldson & Axel Scheffler
books.style: A rhyming voyage where a tiny snail sees the wide world on a whale's tail and proves the small can do great things. Sweeping imagery, steady rhyme, big-hearted.
books.sample: This is the tale of a tiny snail and a great big, grey-blue humpback whale.

books.title: Room on the Broom
books.author: Julia Donaldson & Axel Scheffler
books.style: A rhyming ride where a kind witch makes room for one friend after another, and their teamwork saves the day. Cumulative, rhythmic, warm about kindness.
books.sample: Down fell the hat, and down fell the bow. Is there room on the broom for a dog like me?

books.title: Each Peach Pear Plum
books.author: Janet & Allan Ahlberg
books.style: A rhyming I-spy that hides familiar nursery characters on each page. Short, musical couplets that invite the child to look and find.
books.sample: Each Peach Pear Plum, I spy Tom Thumb.

books.title: The Tiger Who Came to Tea
books.author: Judith Kerr
books.style: An ordinary afternoon turned wonderfully strange when a big friendly tiger drops in and eats everything. Cozy domestic detail, matter-of-fact magic, gentle humor.
books.sample: And then the doorbell rang. It was a big, furry, stripy tiger.
